---
filters:
 - flourish
---

# Writing Simple Functions in R

::: callout
## Learning Objectives

- Understand the components of a function
- Outline how function arguments are used
- Contrast arguments with and without default values
- Illustrate how control flow can be used inside the body of a function
- Explain the differences between explicit and implicit function returns
:::

<!-- Functions they've seen so far: -->

<!-- typeof() -->

<!-- as.character() -->

<!-- as.numeric -->

<!-- c() -->

<!-- matrix -->

<!-- list  -->

<!-- data.frame -->

<!-- seq() -->

<!-- sqrt() -->

<!-- length() -->

<!-- sum() -->

<!-- case_when() -->

<!-- print() -->

At this point, you've encountered almost 20 functions either built-in to R 
(e.g., `sqrt()`) or that live in specific packages (e.g., `case_when()`). For
each function, we've learned how we can use it to get the results we were after,
but we haven't dug into what makes a function a function. That's what this
reading is about!

## Motivation

It is common in statistics and data science to need to rescale a variable before
including it in a model. Rescaling is quite helpful when variables are on very
different scales. In fact, for some machine learning methods (e.g., K-nearest
neighbors) it is critical that **every** variable be on the same scale for an
analysis.

Take for example, some common measures of the economy. The consumer price index
(CPI) measures the yearly change in price for a representative basket of goods
and services[^03-functions-1] and is recorded as a weighted average of prices 
(e.g., \$250). Alternatively, the PPI (producer price index) measures the yearly
change in prices paid to domestic producers of goods and services (e.g., \$120).
Finally, the PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures) is a slightly broader
measure than CPI in that it attempts to measure both how much households are
spending (e.g., \$14,000) and what they're spending their money on.

[^03-functions-1]: [The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS)](https://www.bls.gov/cpi/questions-and-answers.htm) has a database of over
80,000 prices which they sample monthly for more than 200 categories of products
and services to determine the CPI and inflation rate. The BLS uses a huge basket
because its goal is to get an accurate measure of price changes for consumer
goods and services across the U.S. economy.

<!-- Data obtained from https://fred.stlouisfed.org/ -->

:::::::::: columns
::: {.column width="23%"}
```{r}
#| label: economy-year

year <- c(2020, 
          2021, 
          2022, 
          2023, 
          2024, 
          2025)
```
:::

::: {.column width="2%"}
:::

::: {.column width="23%"}
```{r}
#| label: economy-cpi

cpi <- c(258.811, 
         270.970, 
         292.655, 
         304.702, 
         313.689, 
         321.943)
```
:::

::: {.column width="2%"}
:::

::: {.column width="23%"}
```{r}
#| label: economy-ppi

ppi <- c(119.24, 
         119.46, 
         127.82, 
         139.98, 
         142.80, 
         146.16)
```
:::

::: {.column width="2%"}
:::

::: {.column width="25%"}
```{r}
#| label: economy-pce

pce <- c(14231.33,
         16119.67,
         17690.02,
         18833.15,
         19896.01,
         20954.86)
```
:::
::::::::::

Since we have economic measures for each year[^03-functions-2], let's assemble
them into a dataframe:

[^03-functions-2]: If we have vectors that are the **same** length, then each
vector can be a column of a dataframe. If they were *different* lengths we would
need to use a list to store them all together!

```{r}
economy <- data.frame(year = year, 
                      cpi = cpi, 
                      ppi = ppi, 
                      pce = pce)

economy
```

Notice how each of these variables are on different scales? Well, that's exactly
why we might want (need) to rescale them!

### Min-Max Scaling

One common method for rescaling is called "min-max scaling." For any numeric
vector, this method finds the maximum value and the minimum value and then uses
these values to rescale each value of the vector. Mathematically, each $i^{th}$
value of a vector $x$ is scaled based on this calculation:

$$\frac{x_i - min(x)}{max(x) - mix(x)}$$ For example, if we have a vector `x`
with values `c(1, 2, 5, 7)`. Then the minimum of `x` is `1` and the maximum of
`x` is `7`. We then use these values to rescale every value of `x`. This would
look like the following code:

```{r}
#| label: example-min-max

x <- c(1, 2, 5, 7)

(x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
```

Notice that the minimum value is now 0 because `1 - 1` (the minimum is 1) is 0
and the maximum value is now 1 because `(7 - 1) / (7 - 1)` (the maximum is 7) is
1.

We could rescale every column of `economy` using the same code as above:

```{r}
#| label: repeated-rescaling
#| eval: false

economy$cpi <- (economy$cpi - min(economy$cpi)) / (max(economy$cpi) - min(economy$cpi))
economy$ppi <- (economy$ppi - min(economy$ppi)) / (max(economy$ppi) - min(economy$pci))
economy$pce <- (economy$pce - min(economy$pci)) / (max(economy$pce) - min(economy$pce))

```

There are a couple of issues with this process. By repeating this same process
multiple times, it is harder to understand what the code is doing **and** we've
made our code more error prone. Did you notice the error we made?

A better method would be to write a function that allows us to implement this
process anytime we want to rescale a numeric vector!

### Don't Repeat Yourself

The critical motivation behind writing your own functions is the "don't repeat
yourself" (DRY) principle. In general, "you should consider writing a function
whenever copied and pasted your code **more than twice** (i.e. you now have 
three copies of the same code)" (Wickham & Grolemund, 2020).

One of our favorite papers, *Best Practices for Scientific Computing*,
summarizes this idea in a slightly different way:

> Anything that is repeated in two or more places is more difficult to maintain.
> Every time a change or correction is made, multiple locations must be updated,
> which increases the chance of errors and inconsistencies. To avoid this, 
> programmers follow the DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) Principle.

The DRY Principle advocates that you *modularize* your code---break it into
smaller, independent, and reusable units. Modularizing your code helps you 
understand what your code is doing and makes your code more easily re-purposed
for other projects.

## Functions

We've been using functions for weeks now, but we haven't dug deep into what
makes a function a function.

### Syntax

The first hurdle to learning to write a function in a programming language is 
figuring out what syntax you should use. In R, a function is defined using
`function()` and has the following syntax:

```{r}
#| label: function-syntax

rescale <- function(x){
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}
```

Let's break this down into pieces:

![](images/03-function-syntax.png){fig-alt="Basic syntax of a function in R. The function 'rescale' is assigned using '<-' to 'function(x)'. The function has one argument, x, which is dilineated with a red arrow and an annotation stating 'function argument (input)'. The body of the function is enclosed in curly brackets which are circled with dashed black lines. Inside the brackets, there is a single line of code '(x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))' which is higlighted in orange with an annotation stating that this code represents the 'function body'."} </br>

**Function Name:** When you are reading the code above, the first thing you see
is the **name** of the function (`rescale`). This name is chosen by the user, so
we could have equally chosen to name the function `bananna`. However, that seems
like a bad name since it doesn't indicate what the goal of the function is. In
general, we advocate to [use verbs as function names](https://style.tidyverse.org/functions.html#naming) where the verb
describes what the function does.

**Function Arguments:** Inside the `()` after `function` is where the
*arguments* (inputs) of the function are defined. Here we have one argument
(`x`), but we will learn how to incorporate more than one argument.

**Function Body:** After the `function(x)` there is a `{` which starts the 
*body* of the function. The body of the function is everything contained between
the opening `{` and the closing `}`.

<!-- [^2: This type of function is not the only type in R: they are called closures (a name with origins in LISP) A closure has three components, its formals (its argument list), its body (expr in the ‘Usage’ section) and its environment which provides the enclosure of the evaluation frame when the closure is used.] -->

**Beginning & Ending the Function:** Similar to other code styling principles
we've learned, the guideline is for the opening `{` to be the *last* character
on the line and for the closing `}` to be placed on its own line. These 
guidelines are to make your code easier to read / understand. The code below
accomplishes the same task as above but is harder to read / understand what is
happening:

```{r}
#| label: bad-function-style
#| eval: false

rescale <- function(x){ (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x)) }
```

::: callout-check-in
1.  Now that we have created the `rescale()` function, let's check our intuition
for what the function should output (return) for different inputs.

```{r}
x <- 3
y <- 10:20
z <- c(1, 4, 5, 6, "dog")
```

a.  What would you expect `rescale()` to output when `x` is input?
b.  What would you expect `rescale()` to output when `y` is input?
c.  What would you expect `rescale()` to output when `z` is input?

<!-- question about rescale being vectorized -->
:::

### Arguments & Parameters

<!-- add discussion of difference between argument and parameter -->

Currently, the `rescale()` function has only one argument (`x`), but what if it
had more? What are other arguments that we might want the function to use?

Well, suppose we had a vector with some missing values (`NA`s):

```{r}
#| label: vector-with-missing-values

nums <- c(15:20, NA, 35:40, NA)  
```

If we were to try and use our `rescale()` function on `nums` we would get:

```{r}
rescale(nums)
```

That doesn't look right! What might be happening here?

When a vector has `NA` values, any mathematical operation (e.g., `+`, `-`) will
result in an `NA` value. This is because R refuses to ignore the missing values
(i.e., treat them as 0) unless the user specifies that is what they want. To
specify that you want to remove the missing values, you need to use the `na.rm`
argument:

```{r}
#| label: na-rm-arg

mean(nums, na.rm = TRUE)
```

Let's incorporate something like this into our function!

#### Removing Missing Values

Similar to the `mean()` function, we could incorporate an `na.rm` argument into
our `rescale()` function. We could allow the user to specify if they wanted
missing values to be removed (`TRUE`) or not (`FALSE`). Our calculations would
then change based on what the user specified:

```{r}
#| label: rescale-with-na-condition

rescale <- function(x, na.rm){
  if(na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  }
}
```

To incorporate this argument we get to use our knowledge of control flow! 
Specifically, we can use `if` and `else` to define two possible options of what
should happen based on the user's input. Our `rescale()` function should now 
work for vectors with missing values!

```{r}
rescale(nums, na.rm = TRUE)
```

However, notice what happens when we don't include an `na.rm` argument:

```{r}
#| label: not-including-required-argument
#| error: true

rescale(nums)
```

So, our function won't run unless the user provides a value to the `na.rm` 
argument. This isn't what the `mean()` function does! Indeed, you can run 
`mean(nums)` and get `r mean(nums)`, which means that there is a 
**default value** for the `na.rm` argument that is used if the user doesn't
specify a value.

If you look at the help file for the `mean()` function (typing `?mean` into your
Console), you will see that by default `na.rm` is set to `FALSE` 
(`na.rm = FALSE`). We can incorporate something like this into our function!

```{r}
#| label: rescale-with-default-na-arg

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE){
  if(na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  }
}
```

Now, if the user doesn't specify a value for `na.rm` it defaults to `FALSE`:

```{r}
#| label: no-longer-required-arg

rescale(nums)
```

#### Choosing a Rescale Method

Another addition we could make to our function is to allow the user to choose 
what rescaling method they want to use. Currently, our function uses min-max
scaling, but there are other methods of scaling a vector of numbers. Arguably
the most common scaling method is "normalization." With normalization, the mean
and standard deviation are used to rescale values.

Mathematically, each $i^{th}$ value of a vector $x$ is scaled based on this
calculation:

$$\frac{x_i - mean(x)}{std(x)}$$

<!-- add an argument that allows the user to choose between min/max and mean/sd scaling -->

What if we add an argument into the `rescale()` function that allows the user to
specify what method should be used to rescale?! It would make the most sense for
the argument to accept a **character** input for the name of the scaling method
(e.g. `"min-max"`, `"normalize"`). Let's see how we might incorporate this!

Before incorporating this option in to the function, let's write out how many 
possible options there will be:

1.  `"min-max"` with `na.rm = TRUE`
2.  `"min-max"` with `na.rm = FALSE`
3.  `"normalize"` with `na.rm = TRUE`
4.  `"normalize"` with `na.rm = FALSE`

There are two things we should notice here. First, since there are four options
of what can happen, we will need to make use of `else if ()` statements. Second,
each of these conditions requires **two** things to be true at the same time.
Let's learn how we can join relational statements together!

::: callout-note
# Joining Relational Statements

When you have two relational statements (e.g., `method =="normalize"`,
`na.rm == TRUE`), there are **two** ways you can join them---an "and" or an 
"or." 

With an "and" (`&`) *both* statements must be `TRUE` simultaneously in order for
the condition to be `TRUE`. If either statement is `FALSE`, then condition will 
return `FALSE`. 

With an "or" (`|`) *at least one* statement must be `TRUE` for the condition to
be `TRUE`. An or is less stringent and will only be `FALSE` if *every* statement
is `FALSE`.

| Relational Statement | When is it `TRUE`?                  |
|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| `x == 2 & y == 4`    | When **both** statements are `TRUE` |
| `x == 2 | y == 4`    | When **one** statement is `TRUE`    |
:::

Okay, now we're ready to try this out. We know we need four total options, so
we're going to use one `if ()`, two `else if ()`, and one `else`. We're also 
going to need to join two relational statements together for each of these 
options. Here we go!

```{r}

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

Now that we've written it, let's see if it works. 

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
rescale(nums, 
        na.rm = TRUE, 
        method = "min-max")
```
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
```{r}
rescale(nums, 
        na.rm = TRUE, 
        method = "normalize")
```
:::
:::

::: callout-check-in

We know `rescale()` works, so let's test our intuition for what would happen
for the following scenarios. 

2. `rescale(nums, method = "normalize")`
3. `rescale(nums, method = "min-max")`
4. `rescale(nums, na.rm = TRUE)`
:::

### Explicit Returns

Now that we've explored different options for function inputs, let's talk more
about function outputs. A function output is often called a "return" because of 
its tie to a `return()` statement. You might wonder why you haven't seen a 
`return()` statement in a function yet and the answer is that `return()`
statements are a somewhat contentious topic among R programmers. We say
"contentious" because many programmers have staunch views of using a `return()`
statement, either for or against. We think you should be aware of these views so
you can decide which option you prefer.

**Option 1**: Using an explicit return

When we say "explicit return" we mean that there is a `return()` statement built in to the body of the function. This would look something like this:

```{r}
#| label: explicit-return

rescale <- function(x){
  x_scaled <- (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  return(x_scaled)
}

```

Notice how the results of the rescaling are saved into an object (`x_scaled`) and then the `return()` statement *explicitly* states that `x_scaled` should be returned (output). Here are a few pros and cons of this method!

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
✅ Pros of Explicit `return()`

- Clarity and explicit intent
- Consistency with other languages

<!-- - Using a `return()` mimics the behavior that other programming languages  -->
<!-- require (e.g., Python).  -->
<!-- - A `return()` statement is easy to see (it is printed as a different color in -->
<!-- RStudio), so it makes it easier to notice if your function is outputting what -->
<!-- you want it to.  -->
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
⚠️ Cons of Explicit `return()`

- More typing / slightly less idiomatic use of R
- Can be overused in simple functions
- Can complicate control flow unnecessarily

<!-- - The R programming language does not require an explicit return. Instead, R -->
<!-- will return the last value that was calculated.  -->
<!-- - This typically means you need to save a object that will then be returned,  -->
<!-- which adds an additional step to the code. -->
:::
::::::

**Option 2**: Not using an explicit return / Using last value calculated

This option effectively removes the `return()` statement from the function. Instead of using a `return()` statement, the function relies on R's behavior to return the last expression that was run.

```{r}
#| label: no-explicit-return

rescale <- function(x){
  (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}
```

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
✅ Pros of Implicit Return (no `return()` statement)

- Concise and idiomatic
- Encourages "last expression" thinking
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
⚠️ Cons of Implicit Return

- Less obvious for beginners
- Harder to do early exits (i.e., your function ends early for certain inputs)
:::
::::::

**Choosing an Option**

As an R programmer, you will need to decide for yourself which of these two
options you prefer. It might be hard to know without trying what option you 
would prefer, so we encourage you to play around and try both!

The key is that you must be mindful to not mix and match methods between the
two. Take for example a classic function writing mistake:

```{r}
#| label: save-object-no-return
#| eval: false

rescale <- function(x){
  x_scaled <- (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
}
```

If we were to try and use this function to rescale a vector (e.g., `rescale(y)`)
nothing would be output! That is because the rescaled values are saved into an
object (`x_scaled`) but never returned!

::: callout-note
# Consistency with code style guide

Technically, the code style guide we've been using takes an anti explicit return
stance. If you want to hear more about their thinking, you can look at [these examples](https://style.tidyverse.org/functions.html#return).
:::

## Debugging

Sometimes function writing doesn't go as smoothly as what we've walked you 
through. Sometimes your function outputs something unexpected or sometimes it
doesn't run at all. This can be very frustrating, but there are a few methods
you can used to try and hunt down the problem. 

### `print()` Statements

Suppose our function wasn't working as intended.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: broken-function-silent

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "minmax" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

```{r}
#| label: wrong-output-broken-function

x <- 10:20
rescale(x, method = "min-max")
```

How do we know it's not working as intended? Well, with min-max scaling, the 
largest value should be 1 and there should be no negative numbers. 

One method for debugging is to add a `print()` statement into the body of the
function. A `print()` statement does exactly what it sounds like, it prints the
value of an object. For example, `print(nums)` would output ``r nums``. Let's
see how this might look. 

Here is the code for the broken function:

```{r}
#| label: broken-function

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "minmax" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

You might have already spotted our error, but let's suppose it was less 
obvious. Since it seems like the incorrect method is being used, we could add a
`print()` statement at the beginning of the function to print out the value of
`method` being input into the control flow. 

```{r}
#| label: broken-function-print

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  print(method)
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "minmax" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

```{r}
#| label: print-statement-incorporated

rescale(x, method = "min-max")
```

Okay, so the value of `method` is what we expect (`"min-max"`), but the output
is still wrong. By knowing the value of `method` we can go back and check where
this value is being used. Doing this, we should notice that in the first 
`else if ()` we've used `"minmax"` instead of `"min-max"`. So, 
`rescale(x, method = "min-max")` was being thrown into the last condition 
(`else`) because `na.rm` was `FALSE` and the `method` didn't match any of the 
values included in the control flow!

### Running the Function Body

Another method for finding the bug in a function is to run the **function body**
line by line. To do this, you first need to create objects that correspond to 
the function's arguments. The `rescale()` function has three arguments 
(`x`, `na.rm`, and `method`), so we will need to create three objects with 
values that should be used. 

```{r}
#| label: function-arguments-set-for-debug

x <- 10:20
method <- "min-max"
na.rm <- FALSE
```

Now that we have objects for each of the function arguments, we can run the 
**inside** of the function. Specifically, we run this code:

```{r}
#| label: function-body-debug
#| error: true

  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(values)) / (max(values) - min(values))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
```

By running the body of the function we can see that the code is using an object
named `values` somewhere. The culprit is again the first `else if ()` line 
where `min(values)` is used instead of `min(x)`. 

## Input validation

When we write functions we know what type of inputs the function should use. 
However, someone else using our function might not know what the function 
expects. We will walk you through a series of input checks from simple to more
complex, 

### Checking a Single Data Type

It might be obvious to us that the `rescale()` function should only be used
with numeric values, but that might not be clear to someone else. We can 
prevent someone from giving our function a non-numeric input by adding a simple
check at the beginning of the function:

```{r}
#| label: one-numeric-check

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  # check if x is a numeric vector
  stopifnot(is.numeric(x))
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

The `stopifnot()` function takes one input, the condition you want to check. 
Here, we are checking if `x` is a numeric vector. The `is.numeric()` function 
will output two possible values, `TRUE` and `FLASE`. If the output is `FALSE`, 
then `stopifnot()` will terminate the function. 

```{r}
#| label: rescale-characters
#| error: true

letters <- c("a", "b", "c", "d")
rescale(letters, method = "min-max")
```

<!-- Allison: I liked this, but for some reason the same thing is not happening with  -->
<!-- the stopifnot() for a scalar x, so I removed it. -->

<!-- Notice how the function outputs an error before it even gets to the function  -->
<!-- body? Technically, `rescale(letters)` should have produced an error because the -->
<!-- argument `method` wasn't specified. But, the function never even got there!  -->

### Checking Multiple Data Types

In a similar vein, we could check **multiple** data types still using the
`stopifnot()` function. We also know the `na.rm` argument should be a logical 
value, and the `method` argument should be a character value. Let's add these
checks into the function!

```{r}
#| label: three-data-type-input-checks

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  # check if all inputs are correct data type
  stopifnot(is.numeric(x), 
            is.logical(na.rm), 
            is.character(method)
            )
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

You might wonder why we don't have three `stopifnot()`s, one for each condition:

```{r}
#| label: multiple-stop-statements

stopifnot(is.numeric(x)) 
stopifnot(is.logical(na.rm))
stopifnot(is.character(method))

```

That is a great question! When you give `stopifnot()` multiple inputs
(e.g., `stopifnot(is.numeric(x), is.logical(na.rm)`), **every**
input must be `TRUE` simultaneously for the function to run. If *any* of the
inputs are `FALSE` the function will stop. 

This is the same as joining these statements with an `&`:

```{r}
stopifnot(is.numeric(x) & is.logical(na.rm) & is.character(method))
```

So, we use the three inputs (separated by commas) because (1) it requires 
the least amount of typing (instead of three `stopifnot()`s), and (2) it is 
easier to read (instead of using `&`s). 

Let's see if it works! Here are a few unexpected, but reasonable inputs:

```{r}
#| label: rescale-mult-input-check-trial
#| error: true

rescale(10:20, na.rm = "true", method = "min-max")
rescale(10:20, na.rm = TRUE, method = 2)

```


### Checking Object Size

Another common input check is to verify that the object(s) being input are the 
correct size. For example, our `rescale()` function expects `x` to be a vector
with at least two values. Why? When calculating the standard deviation of `x`, 
if the vector has only one value the standard deviation is an `NA`:

```{r}
#| label: sd-na-atomic-vector

x <- 5
sd(x)
```

To prevent this from happening, we can incorporate one additional check into 
our `rescale()` function. Specifically, we can check the `length()` of `x` and 
verify that it has more than 1 element:

```{r}
#| label: add-input-check-for-object-size

rescale <- function(x, na.rm = FALSE, method){
  
  # check if all inputs are correct data type
  # and x has at least two elements
  stopifnot(is.numeric(x), 
            is.logical(na.rm), 
            is.character(method), 
            length(x) > 1
            )
  
  if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / (max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE))
  } else if (method == "min-max" & na.rm == FALSE) {
    (x - min(x)) / (max(x) - min(x))
  } else if (method == "normalize" & na.rm == TRUE){
    (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  } else{
    (x - mean(x)) / sd(x)
  }
}
```

Let's see if it works!

```{r}
#| label: rescale-scalar-error
#| error: true

x <- 5
rescale(x, method = "min-max")
```


<!-- checkin about verifying the length of x is greater than 1 -->
::: callout-check-in

We've incorporated a input check for the size of `x`, but not for the size of
`na.rm` or `method`. 

5. What `length()` should we expect `na.rm` to have?

6. What `length()` should we expect `method` to have?

7. What should the final set of input checks be?

```{r}
#| label: all-input-checks
#| eval: false
#| flourish:
#| - target: 
#|     - "is.numeric"
#|     - mask: true
#|     - style: "text-decoration: underline;"
#| - target: 
#|     - "is.logical"
#|     - mask: true
#|     - style: "text-decoration: underline;"
#| - target: 
#|     - "is.character"
#|     - mask: true
#|     - style: "text-decoration: underline;"
#| - target: 
#|     - "blah"
#|     - mask: true
#|     - style: "text-decoration: underline;"

  stopifnot(is.numeric(x), 
            is.logical(na.rm), 
            is.character(method), 
            blahblahblah > 1, 
            blahblahblah == 1, 
            blahblahblah == 1
            )
```
:::

## Environment, Scope, Name Masking & Dynamic Lookup

<!-- give examples and ask them to identify where the values are pulled from? -->

<!-- argument order versus names -->
